rankings.blurb: Week 1 result: W 12-2-2 vs Caminero Crush. Twelve. Categories. To. Two. The most dominant performance of the opening week by a country mile. Vlad Jr. hit .318 with 10 homers and 27 RBI across the matchup — an OBP of .441 that made Caminero Crush's pitching staff look like a batting practice machine. Chris Sale, Kirby, and Woo combined for a 2.36 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP and nearly four strikeouts for every walk. This team won literally every category except W and K, and it wasn't close in any of them. The long-term projection still shows a team with speed issues (86 SB, worst in the league) and a run-production ceiling that won't scare top offenses. But right now? This is the team nobody wants to play. Sale looks like vintage Sale, the K/BB ratio (3.92 in Week 1, 4.22 projected) is a weapon, and Arraez is going to hit .310 in his sleep. Week 1 was a masterclass.

Week 1 result: W 10-3-3 vs PO' BOYD SANDWICH. Ten categories won. A 3.02 ERA. An OBP of .385 that had PO' BOYD's pitchers questioning their career choices. Ohtani, Acuña, Tatis, and Elly De La Cruz combined for 6 homers and the kind of stat line that makes projection systems blush. Cole and Glasnow showed up on the mound with a 3.50 K/BB that anchored the pitching side too. This team dominated both sides of the ball in Week 1, which was NOT supposed to happen — the pre-season projections had this as a coin flip. The SV+H situation remains a crisis (zero saves-plus-holds in Week 1, 1.2 projected for the season — that is literally nothing), and PO' BOYD did manage to steal the WHIP and SV+H categories. But everything else? Blowout. Wait...wait...wait...wait — this team won the ERA category with a bullpen that consists of prayers and Payton Tolle? That's not sustainable. But it happened. And right now, momentum is a real thing.

Week 1 result: L 6-7-3 vs Duran Duran. The pre-season number one team, the 11-0 projected juggernaut, the team that was supposed to beat EVERY SINGLE TEAM in the league... lost its very first matchup. An 8.03 ERA. A 1.70 WHIP. From the team that was projected to post a 3.30 ERA. Crochet and Webb apparently forgot how to pitch for one week, and Duran Duran's bullpen (Díaz, Duran, Jansen, Estévez) just picked them apart on the pitching side while JRam and company out-RBI'd them. The hitting was still fine — .225 AVG, .327 OBP, Judge and Soto did their thing — but when your pitching posts an 8.03 ERA, you're not winning pitching categories against anyone. Here's the thing though: the long-term projections STILL have this as the best roster in the league. Judge and Soto are still going to hit 90+ combined homers. The reliever strategy still works in theory. This was one bad week of small-sample pitching variance. Drop from 1 to 3 is a reality check, not a funeral. But it IS a reality check.

Week 1 result: W 7-5-4 vs Nine Spoons One Brûlée. A solid, workmanlike win powered by a .279 team AVG and a .380 OBP that made Nine Spoons' pitching staff look significantly less elite than advertised. Bobby Witt Jr. and Lindor set the table, and the offense outscored Nine Spoons 22-20 in runs and 21-18 in RBI. The pitching wasn't spectacular (4.50 ERA, 1.50 WHIP) but it didn't need to be — the bats carried. The SV+H edge (4-0) was a quiet dagger. This is still the most balanced roster in the league: deep lineup, Wheeler-Gilbert rotation, and enough bullpen to compete everywhere. The 7-5-4 win doesn't scream dominance, but it screams consistency, and in a 25-week season, consistency is how you make the playoffs. Drops two spots only because Señor Burns and CLAUDE 4 COMMISH had monster weeks.

Week 1 result: L 6-8-2 vs Camp of the New Orleans Saints. Lost the matchup but posted a 2.18 ERA and a 0.91 WHIP, which are numbers that belong in a museum, not a losing effort. The pitching was absolutely filthy — Strider, Hunter Greene, and the boys just mowed people down — but the offense hit .224 with a .300 OBP, which is the batting equivalent of bringing a spork to a steakhouse. Camp of the New Orleans Saints hit .295 against them and that was the ballgame. Here's what matters going forward: if this team's bats wake up even slightly — and with Devers, Machado, Bregman, and Riley Greene in the lineup, they should — the pitching is already elite enough to carry them into the top three. 1,305 projected strikeouts. A 2.18 ERA in Week 1. This roster has the highest ceiling in the league outside of Universal Basic Civale. The Week 1 loss was an offensive aberration. The pitching is very, very real.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Week 1 result: W 8-6-2 vs Balls to the Wall. A .295 team batting average. A .361 OBP. Tucker and Seager absolutely feasted, and this team won every single hitting category except HR and SB. That's the good news. The bad news is the pitching posted a 5.70 ERA and a 1.34 WHIP, and the only reason they won 8 categories is because the bats were so scorching hot they covered for the mound's sins. Also — and I cannot stress this enough — 0.6 projected saves-plus-holds for the entire season. Zero in Week 1. That's not a weakness, that's a surrender. Every single week this team starts 0-1 in SV+H before anyone throws a pitch. Tucker is a top-5 fantasy bat and Seager is a machine, but the complete absence of a bullpen caps this team's ceiling at 'good, not great.' Unless they make a trade for relievers, this is a 6th-place team with 4th-place bats.

Week 1 result: W 7-6-3 vs Universal Basic Civale. Beat. The. Number. One. Team. In. The. League. Week one. First matchup of the season. Duran Duran took down the pre-season juggernaut by winning 7 categories including RBI, SB, K, ERA, WHIP, QS, AND SV+H. JRam drove in 16 and the bullpen quartet of Díaz, Duran, Jansen, and Estévez held down a 5.57 ERA that was somehow elite compared to UBC's 8.03 disaster. Wait...wait...wait...wait — Duran Duran posted 41 strikeouts to UBC's 19? From a team with 27 projected quality starts for the whole year? That's not sustainable but it IS hilarious. The long-term projections still say this is a bottom-half team because the starting pitching (Williams, Rogers, Horton) is objectively thin and the 2.83 K/BB ratio will regress. But for one glorious week, the bullpen-and-bats strategy worked to perfection. Enjoy it. Frame it. Tell your grandchildren.

Week 1 result: W 8-5-3 vs The Polar Express. The 0-11 projected team. The roster that was supposed to show up with a pool noodle. Won its first matchup. Beat a team that was projected 5 spots higher. Posted a 3.58 ERA and racked up 53 strikeouts to The Polar Express's 35. Hunter Brown was the best pitcher on the field. Ryan Helsley collected saves. The bats hit .235, which isn't sexy, but it was better than The Polar Express's .215, and sometimes fantasy baseball is just about being less bad than the other guy. The projections still hate this team — 0-11 against the field, the Woodruff/Ray/Alcantara injury trifecta looms over everything — but the kid showed up to the sword fight with the pool noodle and somehow won the duel. Zac Gallen is a real arm, Helsley and Hoffman anchor the pen, and the prospect stash (Walker Jenkins, Jordan Lawlar, Kyle Teel) means the future is bright even if the present is shaky. Jumped four spots on the strength of one genuinely encouraging week.

Week 1 result: L 5-7-4 vs Young and Yandy Indiana Drift. The pitching that was supposed to dominate — Skenes, Ohtani (P), Ragans — posted a 5.59 ERA. Against a team that hit .279 with a .380 OBP, you need your pitching to show up, and it simply didn't. The offense managed a .206 batting average, which is the kind of number that makes you wonder if the bats were delivered to the wrong stadium. Gunnar Henderson did Gunnar Henderson things but the supporting cast (Goodman, Soderstrom, Marsee, Rafaela) combined for the kind of stat line you'd expect from a Tuesday night beer league. Zero saves-plus-holds. Zero wins on the mound. The long-term projections still like the pitching (1,138 K, 3.44 ERA projected) and Henderson is a franchise cornerstone, but the 643 projected RBI is a structural problem no amount of strikeouts can fix. This team drops four spots because the Week 1 performance confirmed the pre-season fear: the offense is genuinely bad.

Week 1 result: L 5-8-3 vs ShawSwank Redemption. Lost to the last-place projected team. Hit .215 with a .279 OBP. Posted a 5.88 ERA. Yamamoto, Valdez, and Eovaldi combined for the kind of stat line that makes you want to check if the projections were generated by a random number generator. The offense scored 15 runs and hit 3 homers, which would be respectable for a three-day work week but is underwhelming for a full fantasy week. Hader and Devin Williams won the K/BB category (3.89) but that was about the only bright spot. The biggest drop in the rankings this week — from 3 to 10 — reflects both the ugly Week 1 AND the fact that several teams above them had monster performances. The long-term projections (71 W, 71 QS, 96 SV+H, Yamamoto-Valdez-Eovaldi rotation) still suggest this is a top-half team. But right now they look like a team that got off the train at the wrong stop.

Week 1 result: L 3-10-3 vs CLAUDE 4 COMMISH. Got demolished. A .198 batting average. A .305 OBP. Three homers for the entire week. The offense looked like it was assembled from the clearance rack at a fantasy baseball yard sale — which, to be fair, it kind of was. Alvarez and Schwarber are elite bats but the surrounding cast (Ben Rice, Addison Barger, Luke Keaschall) is the kind of lineup that makes you squint at the screen and say 'wait, really?' The one bright spot was a 1.09 WHIP from the pitching staff, but even that wasn't enough because the bats produced literally nothing. The SP depth (Fried, Bradish, King, Rodón, Peralta — many still on the bench) remains the single most valuable untapped resource in the league. If those arms get activated, the pitching projections (78 W, 78 QS, 1,128 K) are legitimately elite. But the offense needs help. Desperately.

Week 1 result: L 2-12-2 vs Señor Burns. Two categories won. Out of sixteen. Against a team that hit .318 with a .441 OBP and 27 RBI. Señor Burns treated this matchup like batting practice and Caminero Crush was the ball. The pitching was actually decent — a 2.36 ERA, a 1.01 WHIP, Skubal looked like the reigning Cy Young — but it didn't matter because the offense hit .240 and produced 17 RBI to Señor Burns's 27. Julio Rodríguez and Oneil Cruz have the talent to carry a lineup, but the supporting cast (Torkelson, Okamoto, Baldwin) needs several miracles to be league-average. Corbin Burnes on the IL is the ghost in the machine — when he returns, this team's pitching goes from 'Skubal and hope' to 'Skubal, Burnes, and hope,' which is a meaningful upgrade. But right now, this is the cellar. The name changed from Boydz n the Hood to Caminero Crush, which is the fantasy equivalent of rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic.
